parsed.title: Variational Learning Induces Adaptive Label Smoothing
parsed.field: Bayesian deep learning / variational inference / label smoothing / robust learning under label noise
parsed.abstract: The paper claims that variational learning naturally induces an adaptive form of label smoothing where label noise is specialized for each example. The authors derive the form of this induced label noise for logistic regression, generalized linear models (GLM), and neural networks trained with IVON (Improved Variational Online Newton). They argue this connects Bayesian methods to label smoothing and provides a principled way to handle overconfident predictions. Experiments show IVON outperforms standard label smoothing (and is comparable to SAM) on datasets with synthetic and natural label noise.

sections.introduction: Adaptive label smoothing (LS) adjusts per-example label noise and has been used for generalization, mislabeled examples, calibration, and OOD detection. However, designing a good adaptivity strategy is task-specific and ad hoc. The authors propose to skip explicit strategy design by exploiting the natural adaptivity arising from variational learning. They claim variational learning assigns higher noise to atypical examples, behaves similarly to Online Label Smoothing (OLS, Zhang et al. 2021), and that IVON outperforms LS by up to 9% under pair-flip noise and up to ~50% under data-dependent noise.
sections.related_work: Reviews LS literature (Szegedy 2016, Pereyra 2017, Müller 2019, Damian 2021, Lukasik 2020), and adaptive LS strategies based on logits (Park 2023), softmax predictions (Zhang 2021, Ko 2023), entropy (Lee 2022), and OOD detection (Xu 2024). Also notes Bayesian-flavored motivations (Pereyra 2017b, Ghoshal 2021) that use posterior over labels but not over parameters θ.
sections.method: Variational learning minimizes E_q[loss] + KL(q||p) over a Gaussian q(θ). For logistic regression with q = N(θ_t, I), the authors show GD on the variational objective equals standard GD with labels y_i replaced by y_i + ε_{i|t}, where ε_{i|t} = σ(f_i(θ_t)) − E_{q_t}[σ(f_i(θ))]. The result extends to GLMs by replacing σ with A'(f). For Newton's method, VON gives the same noise form but with a learned covariance Σ_t, yielding more adaptive noise. For neural networks, a Taylor approximation yields ε_{i|t} ≈ σ'(f_i(θ_t)) ∇f_i(θ_t) Σ_t^{1/2} e. The authors use IVON, which estimates a diagonal covariance via Adam-like preconditioning.
sections.theory: Theorem 1 establishes that variational GD with q=N(θ_t,I) is equivalent to standard GD with adaptive label noise (Eq. 10). Theorem 2 (appendix) extends this to VON/Newton's method, yielding the same form of noise but with adaptive Σ_t. Derivations use the surrogate-minimization view of Newton's method and known reformulations of VON as inference on a linear model.
sections.experiments: Three sets of experiments: (1) MNIST visualization showing IVON assigns high noise to atypical digits; (2) CIFAR-10/100 comparison of IVON's smoothed labels to LS and OLS; (3) robustness experiments on CIFAR-10/100 with symmetric and pair-flip synthetic noise, data-dependent noise, and Clothing1M with natural noise, comparing IVON to LS (with α swept) and SAM (with ρ swept). Also an ablation comparing learned vs fixed diagonal Σ_t, and Hessian initialization sensitivity for IVON.
sections.results: IVON's induced label noise visually matches OLS and assigns more noise to ambiguous examples. On CIFAR-10/100 with symmetric and pair-flip noise, IVON outperforms the best LS by up to ~9% and is comparable to or better than tuned SAM. On data-dependent noise on CIFAR-10, IVON outperforms LS/SAM at all noise levels and can still learn at κ=0.4–0.5 where baselines collapse to ~10% accuracy (gap up to 51%). On Clothing1M, IVON ≥ LS by ~1% and is comparable to SAM. Learned Σ_t consistently outperforms fixed diagonal Σ_t.
sections.limitations: Authors note that variational learning introduces noise other than label noise (e.g., feature/parameter noise), and they only analyze the label-noise component. SAM-like derivations are possible but not pursued. No explicit limitations section.
sections.conclusion: Variational learning naturally provides adaptive label smoothing without manual design. IVON consistently beats LS and is comparable to SAM without hyperparameter tuning. Bayesian frameworks are suggested as a principled approach for handling label noise and overconfident predictions.

main_claims: Variational learning with a Gaussian posterior implicitly performs adaptive label smoothing whose noise has an explicit closed form (Eq. 10/16).
The induced label noise increases for atypical/ambiguous examples because predictive variance is amplified near decision boundaries.
Newton-like variational methods (VON/IVON) yield more adaptive noise than first-order variational GD due to a learned covariance.
IVON's adaptive noise behaves similarly to Online Label Smoothing (Zhang et al. 2021) but requires no extra design.
IVON outperforms LS and matches/exceeds SAM under symmetric, pair-flip, data-dependent, and natural label noise, without hyperparameter tuning.
key_contributions: A theoretical derivation showing variational GD/Newton updates are equivalent to standard GD/Newton with adaptive per-example label noise.
Explicit form of induced label noise for logistic regression, GLMs, and (via Taylor approximation) neural networks.
Empirical demonstration that IVON's induced smoothing matches OLS qualitatively and improves robustness to label noise on CIFAR-10/100 and Clothing1M.
Connection between Bayesian/variational methods and the adaptive label-smoothing literature.
experimental_results: MNIST (Fig. 3): IVON assigns visibly higher label noise to atypical/unclear digits than to canonical ones; LS noise is constant.
CIFAR-10/100 (Fig. 4): IVON's per-class smoothed-label distributions resemble OLS (Zhang 2021) but with stronger noise; differ qualitatively from uniform LS.
CIFAR-10 symmetric and pair-flip noise (Fig. 5): IVON beats best LS by 4.3% (20% sym), 6.7% (40% sym), 7.8% (20% pair); comparable to peak SAM, but SAM is sensitive to ρ.
CIFAR-100 symmetric and pair-flip noise (Fig. 8): IVON beats best LS by 2.4%/7.8%/9.1% and beats best SAM by 9.9%/13.3% in some settings.
CIFAR-10 data-dependent noise (Fig. 7): IVON outperforms LS/SAM at all noise levels; at high noise (κ=0.4), IVON gains ~51% over LS; learns where baselines fail.
Clothing1M (Fig. 9): IVON ~1% better than best LS; comparable to SAM.
Ablation (Fig. 6): Learned diagonal Σ_t outperforms fixed diagonal Σ_t across noise settings on CIFAR-100.
Hessian init ablation (Fig. 10): IVON's accuracy varies by <10% over h0 ∈ [0.05, 1.0], less sensitive than SAM's ρ.
assumptions_and_limitations: Theoretical results assume Gaussian variational family q with identity (Sec. 3.1) or full (Sec. 3.3) covariance; logistic regression/GLM assumptions.
Neural-network derivation uses a single-sample Monte Carlo + first-order Taylor expansion, ignoring second-order terms; the equivalence is only approximate.
Analysis isolates the label-noise component while variational learning simultaneously introduces feature/parameter noise; predictive behavior is influenced by all noises.
Comparisons focus on LS and SAM; many other label-noise-robust methods (e.g., loss correction, sample selection, mixup, co-teaching) are not directly compared on benchmarks.
IVON still has hyperparameters (Hessian init, learning rate, λ); the paper claims robustness but tunes these per setting (Table 1).
Some hyperparameter ranges (e.g., SAM ρ on CIFAR-100 includes 0.3 where accuracy degrades sharply) raise questions about fairness of 'best LS' / 'best SAM' baselines.
figures_tables: Fig. 1: Conceptual illustration — LS uses constant noise; variational learning assigns higher noise to atypical '6' digit.
Fig. 2: Label-noise magnitude |ε| as a function of mean f_{i|t} for fixed variance — peaks just off zero.
Fig. 3: MNIST high- vs low-noise examples ranked by IVON noise; atypical digits get high noise.
Fig. 4: Smoothed label distributions per class on CIFAR-10/100 comparing LS, OLS, IVON.
Fig. 5: CIFAR-10 accuracy vs α (LS) and ρ (SAM) at three synthetic noise settings; IVON shown as constant line.
Fig. 6: Learned vs fixed diagonal Σ_t on CIFAR-100 noisy datasets.
Fig. 7: CIFAR-10 with data-dependent noise (κ=0.1–0.5); IVON dominates at high noise.
Fig. 9: Clothing1M results vs α and ρ.
Fig. 10: IVON sensitivity to Hessian initialization on CIFAR-100.
equations_algorithms: Eq. 1: Standard LS y'_i = (1-α)y_i + α u.
Eq. 5: OLS noise ε_{i|t} = α(ū − y_i) using accumulated softmax predictions.
Eq. 7: Variational objective L(q) = Σ E_q[ℓ_i(θ)] + KL(q||p).
Eq. 10: Induced label noise for logistic regression: ε_{i|t} = σ(f_i(θ_t)) − E_{q_t}[σ(f_i(θ))].
Eq. 14: Taylor approximation of ε_{i|t} ≈ σ'(f_{i|t}) (φ_i^T φ_i)^{1/2} e.
Eq. 16: GLM generalization ε_{i|t} = A'(f_i(θ_t)) − E_{q_t}[A'(f_i(θ))].
Eq. 18: VON updates with full Gaussian covariance.
Eq. 20: NN-case induced noise via Taylor expansion: ε_{i|t} ≈ σ'(f_i(θ_t)) ∇f_i(θ_t) Σ_t^{1/2} e.
Algorithm 1: IVON pseudo-code with Adam-like preconditioning and diagonal Hessian estimate.

parsed.field_and_subfield: Bayesian deep learning / variational inference; robust learning under label noise; adaptive label smoothing.
parsed.problem_addressed: Designing adaptive label-smoothing strategies is ad hoc and task-specific. The paper argues that variational learning implicitly performs adaptive label smoothing, avoiding the need to hand-design such strategies.
parsed.research_question: Does optimizing a Gaussian variational objective implicitly induce a principled, per-example adaptive label noise, and if so, does the resulting algorithm (IVON) match or outperform explicit adaptive label-smoothing methods—especially under label noise?
parsed.one_paragraph_summary: The paper draws an explicit connection between variational learning and adaptive label smoothing. By taking gradients of the expected loss under a Gaussian posterior q(θ), the authors show that variational GD on logistic regression is equivalent to standard GD with labels y_i replaced by y_i + ε_{i|t}, where ε_{i|t} = σ(f_i(θ_t)) − E_{q_t}[σ(f_i(θ))]. They extend this to GLMs (replacing σ with A') and, via a first-order Taylor approximation, to neural networks trained with IVON (a diagonal-covariance VON variant). Conceptually, this label noise grows for examples whose predictions sit near the decision boundary and have high predictive variance, naturally yielding more noise for atypical/ambiguous examples. Empirically, on MNIST IVON visibly assigns more noise to atypical digits; on CIFAR-10/100 its per-class smoothed-label distributions resemble Online Label Smoothing (Zhang et al., 2021); and across CIFAR-10/100 (symmetric, pair-flip, data-dependent noise) and Clothing1M, IVON consistently beats best-tuned LS and is comparable to best-tuned SAM, all without sweeping a smoothing hyperparameter.
parsed.problem_statement: Adaptive label smoothing has shown benefits for handling labeling errors, miscalibration, and OOD detection, but each existing strategy is hand-designed for a particular issue (logits, softmax, entropy, etc.). The authors ask whether one can avoid this design step entirely by relying on a method that naturally produces per-example adaptive smoothing—specifically, variational learning with a Gaussian posterior—and whether this approach is competitive with both standard LS and modern robust-training methods such as SAM.
main_contributions: Theorem 1: Gradient descent on the variational objective with q = N(θ_t, I) for logistic regression is exactly equivalent to standard GD with adaptive per-example label noise ε_{i|t} = σ(f_i(θ_t)) − E_{q_t}[σ(f_i(θ))].
Extension of this equivalence to general GLM losses (replacing σ with A') and to Newton/VON updates with a learned full covariance Σ_t, yielding noise that is adaptive in both location and spread.
Approximate derivation (via single-sample MC + first-order Taylor) of the label noise induced by IVON for neural networks: ε_{i|t} ≈ σ'(f_i(θ_t)) ∇f_i(θ_t) Σ_t^{1/2} e.

Empirical demonstration that IVON's induced noise (i) is higher on atypical examples (MNIST), (ii) qualitatively matches Online Label Smoothing on CIFAR-10/100, and (iii) yields robust gains over LS and tuned SAM on CIFAR-10/100 with synthetic, data-dependent, and natural (Clothing1M) label noise.
Ablation evidence that learned diagonal Σ_t is necessary (vs. a fixed diagonal) and that IVON is less sensitive to its main hyperparameter (Hessian init) than SAM is to ρ.

claim_evidence_map.claim: Variational learning is equivalent to GD/Newton on the original loss with adaptive per-example label noise.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Theorem 1 and its proof in Sec. 3.1 (Eqs. 9–11) for logistic regression with q=N(θ_t, I); Sec. 3.2 extension to GLMs (Eq. 16); Theorem 2 and App. A.1 for VON with full covariance via the surrogate-minimization view of Newton's method (Eqs. 23–25).

claim_evidence_map.claim: The induced label noise is larger for atypical/ambiguous examples.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Analytic argument that σ' peaks near 0 and the feature norm controls variance (Sec. 3.1, Eqs. 13–14, Fig. 2); MNIST visualization in Fig. 3 showing that the highest-noise examples are visually unclear digits and lowest-noise are canonical ones.

claim_evidence_map.claim: Newton/VON-style variational learning gives more adaptive noise than first-order variants.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Sec. 3.3 argues Σ_t is learned and gives example-specific predictive variances (Eq. 19); Fig. 6 ablation shows learned diagonal Σ_t outperforms fixed diagonal Σ_t on CIFAR-100 with three noise settings.

claim_evidence_map.claim: IVON's smoothed labels behave similarly to Online Label Smoothing (Zhang et al. 2021).
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Fig. 4 compares per-class smoothed-label distributions for LS, OLS (α=0.5), and IVON on selected CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 classes; IVON and OLS distributions are visually similar (log scale), though IVON's noise tends to be stronger.

claim_evidence_map.claim: IVON outperforms tuned LS and matches or beats tuned SAM under label noise, without smoothing hyperparameter tuning.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Fig. 5 (CIFAR-10 symmetric 20/40%, pair-flip 20%): IVON beats best LS by 4.3/6.7/7.8%. Fig. 8 (CIFAR-100): gains of 2.4/7.8/9.1% over LS and 9.9/13.3% over SAM in some settings. Fig. 7: on CIFAR-10 data-dependent noise, IVON gains up to ~51% at κ=0.4 where LS/SAM degrade to ~10%. Fig. 9: ~1% gain on Clothing1M and parity with SAM.

claim_evidence_map.claim: IVON is less sensitive to its main hyperparameter than SAM is to its perturbation radius.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Fig. 10 shows accuracy varies <10% over Hessian init h_0 ∈ [0.05, 1.0] on CIFAR-100 noisy datasets, while SAM's accuracy in Figs. 5, 8, 9 varies sharply with ρ (e.g., collapses at ρ=0.2–0.3).

claim_evidence_map.claim: The neural-network derivation is only approximate.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Sec. 3.4 explicitly uses a single-sample Monte Carlo and a first-order Taylor expansion, dropping second-order terms; the resulting Eq. 20 is presented with ≈, and the authors note other noise types (e.g., on ∇f) are also introduced but not analyzed.

sections.name: Abstract
sections.summary: States that variational learning naturally induces adaptive label smoothing where label noise is specialized per example. Claims IVON outperforms LS and yields adaptivity similar to existing approaches, connecting Bayesian methods to label smoothing.
key_claims: Variational learning naturally induces adaptive per-example label noise.
IVON outperforms traditional LS and induces noise similar to an existing adaptive approach.
supporting_evidence: Forward references to Eq. 10 for the derived noise and Figs. 3/5/7/8 for empirical comparisons.
issues: Abstract does not specify the scope of the theoretical equivalence (e.g., that for NNs it relies on Taylor approximation and only isolates label noise among multiple induced noises).
missing_information: No mention of the assumptions (Gaussian q, fixed identity covariance in the simplest case) or that variational learning also introduces parameter/feature noise.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: Phrase 'naturally induces an adaptive label smoothing' could mislead readers into thinking the equivalence is exact in the deep learning setting.

sections.name: Introduction
sections.summary: Motivates adaptive LS, surveys ad hoc strategies, and proposes using the natural adaptivity from variational learning. Lists three empirical contributions: high noise for atypical examples, similarity to OLS, and improved robustness to label noise.
key_claims: Designing good adaptive LS strategies is task-specific and ad hoc.
Variational learning yields adaptivity for free.
IVON beats LS by up to 9% on pair-flip and ~50% on data-dependent noise.
supporting_evidence: Citations to Park 2023, Zhang 2021, Lee 2022, Ko 2023, Xu 2024.
issues: The headline 9% and 50% gains are presented without context: they correspond to best-tuned LS in narrow settings; the framing slightly overstates the advantage.
The framing that other adaptive LS strategies require 'additional design effort' is partly rhetorical—IVON itself requires tuning Hessian init, lr, λ (Table 1).
missing_information: No clear statement of what is novel vs. known (e.g., that VON yields expected-loss surrogates is established; novelty is the label-noise interpretation).
No mention of related label-noise robust methods other than LS/SAM.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: 'IVON's adaptive label noise behaves similarly to the proposal of Zhang et al. (2021)' — similarity is shown only via per-class smoothed-label histograms in Fig. 4, which is qualitative.

sections.name: Related Work (Section 2: Label Smoothing and Adaptivity Strategies)
sections.summary: Reviews LS variants, adaptive strategies based on logits/softmax/entropy, and prior Bayesian-flavored motivations using posterior over labels rather than over parameters.
key_claims: Existing adaptive LS methods are task-specific and heuristic.
No prior work investigates effectiveness of posterior over θ for label smoothing.
supporting_evidence: Eqs. 3–5 describing OLS update; citations to Pereyra 2017b and Ghoshal 2021.
issues: Claim that 'there are no approaches investigating the effectiveness of posterior over θ' could be challenged; Bayesian deep learning literature (e.g., MC-Dropout, Laplace, deep ensembles) implicitly captures parameter uncertainty effects on training labels via expected losses. The paper does not engage with these.

Discussion of OLS is somewhat brief—e.g., OLS's u is normalized accumulated softmax over the epoch, not the per-sample expected softmax IVON computes; the eventual 'similarity' claim deserves a more precise comparison.
missing_information: Missing discussion of related noise-robust methods (loss correction, mixup, co-teaching, GLS, bootstrapping) that compete with or complement adaptive LS.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: 'Despite their usefulness, clarity of good ways to design adaptive strategy is lacking' — subjective framing without citation/evidence.

sections.name: Method / Theory (Section 3: Variational Learning Induces Adaptive LS)
sections.summary: Derives induced label noise: for logistic regression with q=N(θ_t,I), ε_{i|t} = σ(f_i(θ_t)) − E_{q_t}[σ(f_i(θ))] (Eq. 10). Extends to GLMs (Eq. 16) and Newton's method/VON with learned covariance (Eq. 19). For NNs uses Taylor expansion to derive Eq. 20. Uses IVON in experiments.
key_claims: Theorem 1: variational GD with q=N(θ_t,I) equals GD with adaptive label noise (Eq. 10).
VON yields the same noise form but with adaptive Σ_t (Theorem 2 in appendix).
NN case yields analogous noise via Taylor expansion (Eq. 20).
supporting_evidence: Proof in Sec. 3.1 swapping expectation with derivative, exploiting q with identity covariance.
Appendix A.1 derives VON surrogate via Khan et al. 2019 / Nickl et al. 2024.
issues: Theorem 1 assumes q has fixed identity covariance, which is unusual for variational learning where covariance is also learned. Calling this 'variational learning' is somewhat strong; the result is closer to expected-gradient under Gaussian smoothing.

The 'noise' ε_{i|t} is not zero-mean noise: it is a deterministic function of θ_t (Eq. 10) plus a stochastic component via MC sampling (Eq. 20). The mapping to 'label smoothing' (which traditionally redistributes probability mass) is conceptually loose: the induced 'smoothing' can shift the label arbitrarily and is not constrained to a valid probability simplex.

Sign and normalization: Eq. 10 produces a real-valued ε_{i|t} that, when added to a binary label y_i ∈ {0,1}, may produce values outside [0,1]. Authors do not discuss whether this is a valid 'label smoothing' in any meaningful sense.

For NNs (Eq. 20) the noise is single-sample MC-approximated and treated as label noise even though it contains random parameter noise—this conflates label smoothing with weight perturbation. The paper acknowledges other noises exist but still claims label-smoothing interpretation.
Theorem 1 proof skips justification for moving the gradient inside expectation (only valid because q has fixed mean-parametrization with identity covariance); should be more carefully stated.
Eq. 14 'Taylor approximation does not get better for larger number of samples' is a surprising statement that needs more explanation—why include the approximation then?
The 'adaptivity improves with learned covariance' (Sec. 3.3) is stated without quantitative theory; only Fig. 6 ablation supports it.
missing_information: No discussion of the relationship between this 'label noise' and effective gradient regularization (which is the more standard view of variational learning's effect).
No comparison to existing analyses of variational learning as flat-minima or noise injection methods.
No statement of regularity conditions for swapping ∇ and E (Eq. 11).
No discussion of whether α (smoothing strength) has any analog in the induced noise—how would one control its magnitude?
ambiguous_or_unsupported: Claim that 'second-order methods yield more adaptive label noise than first-order methods, and are expected to perform better in practice' — this is plausible but only loosely supported by Fig. 6.
The phrase 'naturally induces' obscures that the equivalence requires specific algorithmic choices (Gaussian q, particular updates) and approximations for NNs.

sections.name: Experiments (Section 4)
sections.summary: Three groups of experiments: MNIST visualization of induced noise (4.1); comparison with LS/OLS smoothed-label distributions on CIFAR-10/100 (4.2); robustness to symmetric, pair-flip, data-dependent, and natural noise on CIFAR-10/100/Clothing1M (4.3), plus ablations on Σ_t and Hessian init.
key_claims: IVON assigns higher noise to atypical examples (Fig. 3).
IVON's smoothed labels resemble OLS (Fig. 4).
IVON consistently outperforms LS and matches/exceeds SAM under various noise types.
Learned Σ_t outperforms fixed Σ_t (Fig. 6).
IVON is less sensitive to Hessian init than SAM is to ρ (Fig. 10).
supporting_evidence: Figs. 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10; results averaged over 5 seeds in most cases.
Appendix C with hyperparameter details and Table 1.
issues: Fig. 4 'similarity to OLS' is qualitative; no quantitative metric (e.g., KL divergence, correlation) is provided to substantiate the central claim that IVON behaves like OLS.

OLS is shown only in Fig. 4 (visual smoothed-label comparison) but not in the noisy-label robustness experiments (Sec. 4.3). Since OLS is the closest baseline conceptually, its absence in performance benchmarks is a significant gap.

Comparisons are limited to LS and SAM. Many strong noise-robust methods (e.g., GCE, SCE, ELR, co-teaching, DivideMix, mixup, bootstrap) are not compared, especially on Clothing1M where such methods are standard baselines.

Clothing1M result (~1% improvement over LS) is reported with α ranging up to 0.9; baseline LS results appear weak (~65–68%) compared to literature (commonly ~70–74%); raises concerns about baseline strength.
SAM is reported as 'sensitive to ρ' but in Fig. 5 and Fig. 8, SAM's peak performance is sometimes equal to or above IVON; the 'IVON outperforms SAM' claim depends on which ρ is chosen.

Hyperparameters differ across noise settings (Table 1): IVON's lr changes from 0.5 to 0.8, Hessian init from 0.2 to 0.5, weight decay from 2e-4 to 5e-4. Yet the paper claims 'IVON does not need to tune any hyperparameters'—this is contradicted by Table 1.

Pair-flip flip order in CIFAR-10 (airplane→automobile→…→truck→airplane) is an unusual choice that may not match standard pair-flip conventions in the literature (which typically pair semantically similar classes); could affect comparability.
Data-dependent noise (Sec. 4.3.2) is constructed with an arbitrary parametric form (κ + βi); no discussion of why this is representative of realistic data-dependent noise.
'Best LS' is selected from a small α grid {0, 0.1, …, 0.9}; finer grids or other LS variants are not tried.
Fig. 6 ablation tests only three fixed variances (2e-5, 5e-5, 1e-4, 2e-4); range is narrow and biased to small values, possibly underestimating fixed-Σ baseline.
No measurement of calibration (ECE/NLL) despite the paper's framing around overconfidence.
missing_information: No reporting of test NLL, ECE, or other calibration metrics, despite Sec. 1 motivation around overconfidence and calibration.
No comparison to OLS on actual noisy-label accuracy benchmarks.
No standard deviation values reported in figures (only 'over 5 seeds').
No experiments on standard clean benchmarks comparing IVON+LS combinations vs IVON alone.
No exploration of whether explicitly adding LS on top of IVON helps or hurts.
Compute cost comparison between IVON and SAM is not discussed (relevant since IVON requires posterior sampling).
ambiguous_or_unsupported: 'IVON does not need to tune any hyperparameters to perform well' — contradicted by Table 1 and Fig. 10 (which itself shows non-trivial sensitivity, e.g., pair-flip drops sharply for h0=0.05).
Fig. 10 description claims '<10% variation' but the curve for pair-flip 20% noise drops to ~20% at h0=0.05—this is a much larger variation than claimed.
The 'up to 51%' headline gain on data-dependent noise is achieved only at κ=0.4, where both LS and SAM degenerate to near-random; framing it as a general gain overstates the result.

sections.name: Limitations / Conclusion
sections.summary: Concludes that variational learning provides adaptive label smoothing without manual design and that IVON is robust and competitive with SAM. Briefly acknowledges that other noises (feature/parameter) are also induced.
key_claims: Bayesian frameworks are 'naturally suitable' for label noise.
IVON consistently beats LS and matches SAM without hyperparameter tuning.
issues: No dedicated limitations section; acknowledgement of other noise sources is brief and not analyzed.
The conclusion that no hyperparameter tuning is needed is undermined by Table 1 and Sec. C.2.
Broader question of when variational learning helps vs. hurts (e.g., on clean data) is not addressed.
missing_information: Discussion of compute overhead, scalability, applicability beyond image classification.
Limitations of the theoretical analysis (Taylor approximation, single-sample MC, isolation of label noise component) not summarized.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: 'Bayesian frameworks are naturally suitable for label noise' is a broad inference from limited experiments restricted to IVON.

sections.name: Supplementary (Appendix A–C, Algorithm 1)
sections.summary: Provides VON surrogate derivation (A.1), Theorem 2 stating induced noise for Newton's update, IVON pseudo-code (A.2), Hessian init ablation (B.1), and experimental details (C).
key_claims: VON updates equal Newton's update with expected gradient/Hessian.
Induced noise form matches Eq. 26 for VON with full covariance.
Hessian initialization has modest effect on IVON accuracy.
supporting_evidence: Derivation referencing Khan et al. 2019 and Nickl et al. 2024; Algorithm 1 pseudo-code.
issues: Theorem 2's proof is asserted to be 'identical' to Theorem 1's and omitted; given the more complex form (learned Σ_t, exponential family), a full proof should be included.
Fig. 10's claim that accuracy varies by 'up to 10%' is inaccurate for pair-flip 20% noise where accuracy ranges from ~20% (h0=0.05) to ~70% (h0≥0.2)—a ~50% spread.
Hyperparameter tables (Table 1) reveal nontrivial per-setting tuning that contradicts main-text claims of tuning-free behavior.
missing_information: No explicit convergence guarantees or conditions for the theorems.
No appendix justifying single-sample Monte Carlo accuracy or comparing multi-sample variants.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: Statement that IVON's variation '<10%' is contradicted by its own Fig. 10.

claims.claim: Variational learning with q=N(θ_t, I) on logistic regression is exactly equivalent to GD on the original loss with per-example adaptive label noise ε_{i|t} = σ(f_i(θ_t)) − E_q[σ(f_i(θ))].
claims.evidence_in_paper: Theorem 1 with proof in Sec. 3.1 (Eqs. 9–11); derivation uses the reparameterization q(θ)=N(θ_t, I) and standard GLM gradient manipulations.
claims.notes: Proof is short but correct for the specified setup. Note the equivalence holds only with fixed identity covariance and quadratic regularizer; the result is essentially a restatement of how the expected gradient differs from the gradient at the mean.

claims.claim: The equivalence extends to all GLM losses by replacing σ with A'.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Sec. 3.2, Eq. 16; derivation omitted but follows immediately from log-partition function structure of GLMs.
claims.notes: Standard extension; trivially correct given Theorem 1.

claims.claim: VON with full covariance can be seen as a Newton step with noisy labels and the noise has the same form as the GLM case but with adaptive variance Σ_t.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Theorem 2 and App. A.1 (Eqs. 23–26); leverages Khan et al. (2019) and Nickl et al. (2024) surrogate view.
claims.notes: Derivation relies on ρ_t=1 special case; otherwise sound.

claims.claim: For neural networks, IVON induces label noise ε_{i|t} ≈ σ'(f_i(θ_t)) ∇f_i(θ_t) Σ_t^{1/2} e.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Sec. 3.4: uses single-sample MC + first-order Taylor expansion, dropping second-order terms.
claims.notes: Approximation is heuristic; the paper acknowledges it does not improve with more samples (per Sec. 3.1 comment about Taylor approx). No empirical validation that the analytic noise formula matches the actual difference induced by IVON in NN training.

claims.claim: Variational learning assigns higher label noise to atypical/ambiguous examples.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Fig. 3 on MNIST shows high-noise examples are visually atypical digits, low-noise are canonical; analytic argument via σ' peaking near 0 and feature norm (Eqs. 13–14, Fig. 2).
claims.notes: Qualitative MNIST visualization only; no quantitative metric of 'atypicality' correlated with noise magnitude. Single dataset and small CNN.

claims.claim: IVON's smoothed label distributions are similar to Online Label Smoothing (Zhang et al., 2021).
claims.evidence_in_paper: Fig. 4 shows side-by-side log-scale bar plots for selected classes of CIFAR-10 and 10 randomly picked classes of CIFAR-100.
claims.notes: Comparison is purely visual on log scale, which can hide large discrepancies. No quantitative similarity metric (e.g., KL, correlation). 'Surprisingly similar' is subjective.

claims.claim: IVON consistently outperforms tuned Label Smoothing under labeling errors, with up to 9% gains for pair-flip and ~50% for data-dependent noise.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Fig. 5 (CIFAR-10): +4.3/6.7/7.8%; Fig. 8 (CIFAR-100): +2.4/7.8/9.1%; Fig. 7: ~51% at κ=0.4; Fig. 9: +1% on Clothing1M. Results over 5 seeds.
claims.notes: Consistent direction across many settings; the ~50% gain occurs in an extreme regime where baselines collapse to ~10%, which is striking but the comparison fairness depends on training stability. No comparison to specialized noisy-label methods (e.g., Co-teaching, ELR, DivideMix) on Clothing1M.

claims.claim: IVON is comparable to best-tuned SAM but does not require tuning a smoothing/perturbation hyperparameter.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Figs. 5, 8, 9 show IVON near SAM peak; Fig. 10 shows IVON robust to Hessian init.
claims.notes: Mischaracterization: IVON has multiple hyperparameters (learning rate, Hessian init h_0, β_2, λ); Table 1 shows lr and Hessian init were tuned per setting. Claim of 'no hyperparameter tuning' is overstated.

claims.claim: Learned diagonal Σ_t outperforms fixed diagonal Σ_t in IVON.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Fig. 6 on CIFAR-100 with three noise settings shows learned > fixed across 4 fixed variance values.
claims.notes: Single dataset but consistent across noise types.

claims.claim: Connecting Bayesian methods to label smoothing provides a new way to handle overconfident predictions.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Introduction and conclusion frame the contribution; Sec. 2 reviews prior Bayesian motivations (Pereyra 2017b, Ghoshal 2021) and distinguishes them as using only posterior over labels, not over θ.
claims.notes: The connection between expected loss under a posterior and effective label noise is intuitive and arguably implicit in prior weight-noise/variational literature (e.g., Zhang et al. 2018 noisy natural gradient). The framing as 'label smoothing' is the novel contribution rather than the math itself.

claims.claim: Second-order methods yield more adaptive label noise than first-order methods.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Sec. 3.3 argues this conceptually; Fig. 6 provides indirect empirical support via fixed-vs-learned Σ.
claims.notes: No direct comparison between variational GD (fixed I covariance) and IVON on the same NN tasks. Fixed vs. learned diagonal is a weaker ablation than first- vs. second-order.

unsupported_claims: IVON 'does not need to set any such hyperparameters' (Sec. 3.4 and Sec. 4.3.1)—Table 1 shows lr and Hessian init are tuned per setting.

The neural-network-case label-noise formula (Eq. 20) actually approximates the noise IVON induces—no empirical check correlating Eq. 20 with the realized difference between expected and pointwise gradients.
Adaptivity-by-variational-learning addresses the multiple issues motivated in the intro (miscalibration, OOD detection, generalization)—only labeling-error experiments are presented.
overstated_claims: 'IVON does not need to tune any hyperparameters to perform well' (Sec. 4.3.1)—contradicted by Table 1 and the need to set h_0, lr, λ, β_2.
Quantitative similarity between IVON and OLS smoothed labels in Fig. 4 (described as 'surprisingly similar') is shown only via log-scale bar plots on selected classes, with no similarity metric.

Claim that 'variational learning naturally induces' adaptive label smoothing somewhat overstates: the result is an algebraic equivalence between expected and pointwise gradients, conditional on specific variational family choices and (in the NN case) a Taylor approximation.
The intro positions the work as addressing labeling errors, distribution shift, miscalibration, and OOD, but empirical work covers only labeling errors.
claims_requiring_external_verification: Whether IVON's gains over LS hold against modern label-noise-robust baselines (e.g., Co-teaching, ELR, DivideMix, GCE, SOP) on CIFAR-N and Clothing1M, where typical SOTA on Clothing1M is ~74–76%.

Whether Zhang et al. (2021) OLS is the strongest baseline for the smoothed-label-similarity comparison; other adaptive LS methods (Ko et al. 2023; Lee et al. 2022; Park et al. 2023) are mentioned but not compared.

Whether the Newton/VON equivalence claimed via Khan et al. (2019) and Nickl et al. (2024) genuinely yields exact equivalence at ρ_t=1 in the Newton step (App. A.1 derivation relies on these prior results).
Whether SAM with ρ tuned beyond the reported grid matches IVON in the data-dependent and high-noise CIFAR-10/100 regimes.

novelty_axes.claimed_contribution: A closed-form derivation that variational learning with a Gaussian posterior is algebraically equivalent to standard (S)GD / Newton on the original loss but with a per-example adaptive label noise ε_{i|t} = σ(f_i(θ_t)) − E_q[σ(f_i(θ))] (Eq. 10), generalized to GLMs via A'(·) and to NNs via a Taylor approximation.
novelty_axes.why_it_matters: This is the load-bearing theoretical claim — framing variational learning as inducing label smoothing. If the underlying algebraic equivalence was already shown (even implicitly) in the variational/weight-noise literature, then the contribution reduces to a reframing rather than a new theoretical result.
