similar_prior_work.title: Noisy Natural Gradient as Variational Inference
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Shows that natural-gradient updates with injected weight noise are equivalent to variational inference with a Gaussian posterior; the implicit gradient becomes E_q[∇ℓ], exactly the quantity this paper expands into 'gradient at mean plus label noise'.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: Zhang et al. focus on the weight-noise interpretation and on optimizing Gaussian posteriors; they do not algebraically decompose E_q[∇ℓ] into a 'gradient at mean + per-example label-noise' form and do not connect it to the label-smoothing literature.

similar_prior_work.title: Fast and Scalable Bayesian Deep Learning by Weight-Perturbation in Adam (VOGN/Vadam)
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Introduces variational inference algorithms (VOGN, Vadam) that replace pointwise gradients with expected gradients under a Gaussian q — the very quantity the present paper rewrites as a label-noise term.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: Khan et al. interpret the noise as weight perturbation / Bayesian regularization, not as adaptive label smoothing; no closed-form ε_{i|t} expression in label space and no comparison to LS/OLS.

similar_prior_work.title: The Bayesian Learning Rule
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Unifies many learning algorithms (including Newton/VON) as cases of a Bayesian update, providing the surrogate-minimization view of VON used in App. A.1.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: Khan & Rue provide the general framework but do not specifically extract the induced label noise or contrast variational learning with the label-smoothing literature.

similar_prior_work.title: Regularizing Neural Networks by Penalizing Confident Output Distributions
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Motivates label smoothing / confidence penalties using Bayesian error rate, implicitly using a posterior over labels.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: Posterior is over labels y, not over parameters θ; no variational training algorithm and no closed-form adaptive noise.

similar_prior_work.title: Learning Better Structured Representations Using Low-Rank Adaptive Label Smoothing
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Uses a PAC-Bayes argument to motivate adaptive label smoothing.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: Adaptivity is parameterized by a learned low-rank matrix, not induced by a posterior over θ; not derived from a variational objective.

similar_prior_work.title: Variational Learning is Effective for Large Deep Networks (IVON)
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Introduces IVON — the exact algorithm used in the present paper — and shows it competes with Adam/SGD on large models.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: Shen et al. propose IVON as a general optimizer; they do not analyze it as an adaptive label smoother nor benchmark it under symmetric / pair-flip / data-dependent / Clothing1M label noise. The present paper inherits the algorithm and adds the LS interpretation and noisy-label experiments.

similar_prior_work.title: Delving Deep into Label Smoothing (Online Label Smoothing, OLS)
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Adaptive LS strategy where smoothed targets are accumulated softmax predictions; the present paper claims IVON's induced labels look 'surprisingly similar' to OLS.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: OLS is a hand-designed adaptive strategy with an explicit running-average update; the present paper recovers a qualitatively similar smoothing for free as a byproduct of variational learning.

similar_prior_work.title: A Gift from Label Smoothing: Robust Training with Adaptive Label Smoothing via Auxiliary Classifier under Label Noise
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Adaptive LS specifically targeted at label-noise robustness, using auxiliary-classifier predictions to adapt α per example.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: Hand-designed adaptivity using a separate classifier; mentioned in the related-work but not compared empirically in the present paper, even though it directly addresses the same task (label-noise robustness).

similar_prior_work.title: SAM as an Optimal Relaxation of Bayes
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Provides a variational/Bayesian interpretation of SAM, the very baseline this paper compares against.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: Möllenhoff & Khan connect SAM to a Bayes-relaxation but do not extract a label-noise interpretation; the present paper notes a similar derivation for SAM is possible but does not pursue it.

similar_prior_work.title: Does Label Smoothing Mitigate Label Noise?
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Analyzes LS as a regularizer under label-noise, finding mixed behavior; directly relevant to the noisy-label experiments here.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: Lukasik et al. study static LS; the present paper extends to adaptive LS induced by variational learning but does not directly compare to or build on their analysis.

potential_missing_citations: Specialized noisy-label-robust baselines that are routinely used on CIFAR-N/Clothing1M (Co-teaching, Han et al. NeurIPS 2018; DivideMix, Li et al. ICLR 2020; ELR / ELR+, Liu et al. NeurIPS 2020; GCE, Zhang & Sabuncu NeurIPS 2018; SOP, Liu et al. ICML 2022). Their omission means the empirical claims of robustness are evaluated only against LS and SAM, which understates the prior art on this benchmark.
Ko et al. 2023 (AAAI) is cited in related work as an adaptive LS method for label noise but is not compared empirically, even though it targets exactly the experimental setting of Sec. 4.3.
Mukhoti et al. 'Calibrating Deep Neural Networks using Focal Loss' (NeurIPS 2020) for the calibration / confidence-penalty side of the LS literature is not engaged with.

Wenzel et al. 'How Good is the Bayes Posterior in Deep Neural Networks Really?' (ICML 2020) on cold posteriors and the role of label noise in Bayesian DL — directly relevant to the interpretation that variational posteriors interact with label noise.
parsed.rationale: The paper's strongest novelty is conceptual: explicitly reframing variational learning's expected-gradient update as an adaptive label-smoothing scheme with a closed-form per-example noise (Eq. 10/16/20), and connecting two largely separate literatures (Bayesian DL and adaptive LS). This framing — together with the observation that the induced noise qualitatively matches Online Label Smoothing — is, to the best of my knowledge, new. However, the underlying algebra is essentially a restatement of facts that have been implicit in the variational-DL literature for years (Zhang et al. 2018 noisy natural gradient; Khan et al. 2018 VOGN/Vadam; Khan & Rue 2023 Bayesian learning rule), so the *theoretical* contribution is incremental rather than fundamentally new — once one writes E_q[∇ℓ_i(θ)] for a GLM, adding and subtracting σ(f_i(θ_t)) to get a 'noisy label' immediately follows. The method itself is IVON used as-is (Shen et al. 2024). The empirical results are directionally consistent and include a striking 51% gap under data-dependent noise, but the comparison set is restricted to LS and SAM, omitting both the specialized adaptive-LS competitors mentioned in related work (Ko et al. 2023; Lee et al. 2022; Park et al. 2023) and the standard noisy-label-robust baselines (DivideMix, ELR, Co-teaching, GCE, SOP), so the empirical novelty is also incremental. Risk of being preempted is medium — pieces of the argument exist across the Bayesian DL literature, but no prior paper appears to package it as 'variational learning = adaptive label smoothing' with explicit equations and head-to-head comparisons against OLS/LS/SAM under label noise. Overall: a moderately novel conceptual reframing with incremental theoretical and empirical novelty.

domain_expert.add_to_field: Provides a conceptual bridge between Bayesian/variational learning and the adaptive label smoothing literature, showing that variational gradients on a Gaussian posterior implicitly produce per-example label noise of a form similar to existing hand-designed adaptive LS schemes (notably OLS).
domain_expert.beneficiaries: Researchers working on label smoothing, learning with noisy labels, calibration, and Bayesian deep learning; practitioners looking for hyperparameter-light alternatives to LS/SAM.
domain_expert.importance: The problem of ad-hoc adaptive LS strategies is real and recognized in the field, and unifying these under a Bayesian lens is a useful conceptual contribution, even if not transformative.
domain_expert.improvement_meaningful: The reframing is meaningful: it gives a principled derivation for why predictions-based adaptive smoothing works, and connects two largely separate literatures.
domain_expert.future_influence: Likely to motivate further analyses linking variational/PAC-Bayes objectives to specific regularization effects (label noise, weight noise, feature noise) and to spur Bayesian-style baselines in noisy-label benchmarks.

methodology_expert.add_to_field: A clean derivation for logistic regression and GLMs showing that variational GD equals GD on a noisy-label objective, plus a VON/Newton extension where the covariance is learned and contributes to adaptivity. The neural-network case is handled via single-sample MC + first-order Taylor approximation.
methodology_expert.beneficiaries: Theoreticians and algorithm designers interested in variational inference, second-order optimization, and the regularization effects induced by posterior expectations.
methodology_expert.importance: The GLM result is a clear, exact statement; the NN extension is more heuristic. The methodological contribution is moderate rather than deep—the derivations are short and rely on standard manipulations.
methodology_expert.improvement_meaningful: Yes for GLMs (exact equivalence is a useful viewpoint). For NNs, the Taylor argument is a plausibility argument rather than a tight characterization, and other induced noises (on features, Hessian) are acknowledged but not analyzed.
methodology_expert.future_influence: Could inspire more rigorous analyses of which noise components dominate in variational training of deep networks, and tighter connections to loss-correction and PAC-Bayes bounds for noisy labels.

empirical_evaluation_expert.add_to_field: Empirical evidence across MNIST visualizations, CIFAR-10/100 with symmetric, pair-flip, and data-dependent noise, plus Clothing1M, comparing IVON against tuned LS and SAM. Includes ablations on fixed vs. learned Σ_t and Hessian init sensitivity.
empirical_evaluation_expert.beneficiaries: Practitioners evaluating robust training methods on noisy benchmarks; authors of future label-noise papers who may add IVON as a baseline.
empirical_evaluation_expert.importance: Demonstrates consistent and sometimes large gains (up to ~51% on data-dependent noise, 9% on pair-flip), supporting the practical relevance of the connection.
empirical_evaluation_expert.improvement_meaningful: Gains over LS are sizable and the data-dependent noise result is striking. However, comparison to dedicated noisy-label methods (e.g., Co-teaching, DivideMix, loss correction) is absent, and Clothing1M gains over LS are modest (~1%) with parity to SAM.
empirical_evaluation_expert.future_influence: May encourage including variational methods as standard baselines in label-noise benchmarks; the data-dependent noise setup itself is a useful diagnostic.

skeptical_reviewer.add_to_field: An interesting reinterpretation, but much of the 'adaptive label smoothing' framing is one of multiple possible decompositions of the variational gradient; the same expectation could be re-described as weight noise, feature noise, or output smoothing.
skeptical_reviewer.beneficiaries: Mainly readers already sympathetic to Bayesian framings; the practical takeaway largely reduces to 'use IVON instead of SAM/LS for noisy labels.'
skeptical_reviewer.importance: The connection is intuitive but somewhat known in spirit (expected loss under posterior softens predictions). The exact equivalence is only for GLMs with identity covariance; the NN case requires approximations and ignores other induced noises.
skeptical_reviewer.improvement_meaningful: Empirical wins are real but the comparison set is narrow (LS, SAM). No comparison against OLS or other adaptive LS methods on the noisy-label benchmarks, despite OLS being the central conceptual analogue. Clothing1M gains are within noise of SAM.
skeptical_reviewer.future_influence: Modest. Without deeper theory tying the induced noise to specific generalization or calibration guarantees, the contribution may be cited as motivation rather than as a foundational result.

practical_impact_expert.add_to_field: Argues IVON is an effective, near-hyperparameter-free drop-in replacement for LS/SAM under label noise, with the bonus of providing posterior uncertainty.
practical_impact_expert.beneficiaries: Practitioners training on noisy/web-scraped data (e.g., Clothing1M-like scenarios) who want robustness without tuning smoothing rates or perturbation radii.
practical_impact_expert.importance: Hyperparameter robustness is genuinely valuable; the Fig. 10 sensitivity ablation supports this claim relative to SAM's ρ sensitivity.
practical_impact_expert.improvement_meaningful: Yes for small-to-mid-scale benchmarks; less clear at the scale of modern foundation models, which are not evaluated. The data-dependent noise gains are practically compelling.
practical_impact_expert.future_influence: Could nudge practitioners to adopt variational optimizers (IVON) more broadly, especially as Bayesian deep learning toolchains mature.

consensus: The conceptual link between variational learning and adaptive label smoothing is clean and intuitive, and the GLM derivation is correct and clear.
Empirical results consistently favor IVON over tuned LS on noisy-label benchmarks, with notable gains under pair-flip and data-dependent noise.
Reduced hyperparameter sensitivity (vs. SAM's ρ) is a practical advantage.
The NN-level theory is approximate (single-sample MC + first-order Taylor) and only characterizes one of several noise sources induced by variational training.
disagreement: Whether the theoretical contribution is substantive (methodology/domain experts: meaningful unification) or largely a reinterpretation of known facts about expected-loss optimization (skeptic).

Whether empirical comparisons are sufficient: practical-impact and empirical experts see strong evidence, while the skeptic notes the absence of comparisons to OLS and dedicated noisy-label methods on the robustness benchmarks.
Whether the work will be foundational or mainly motivational for future research.

definitions_clear: Label smoothing and its adaptive variants are clearly defined (Eqs. 1, 2, 5).
Variational objective and Gaussian posterior family are explicitly specified (Eq. 7, q_t = N(θ_t, I) or N(θ_t, Σ_t)).
GLM loss and induced label noise formulas are clearly stated (Eqs. 8, 10, 15, 16).
assumptions_clear: Gaussian posterior assumption is stated explicitly.
Quadratic regularizer R_0(θ)=‖θ‖²/2 is stated for the logistic regression derivation.
ρ_t=1 is explicitly used to recover Newton-like updates.
derivations_algorithms_clear: Theorem 1 derivation is clear and short.
Appendix A.1 provides the surrogate derivation for VON via prior work (Khan et al. 2019; Nickl et al. 2024).
suggested_improvements_for_formulation: Clarify that ε_{i|t} is not zero-mean noise and is not constrained to keep y_i + ε_{i|t} in [0,1], so the mapping to 'label smoothing' is conceptual rather than literal.
State regularity conditions for swapping ∇ and E_q in Eq. 11.
Explicitly note that Theorem 1 uses a fixed identity covariance (not the standard 'variational learning' which also learns covariance), and how this restricts the claim's scope.
For the NN case (Eq. 20), make explicit which assumptions are needed for the Taylor approximation and the interpretation of the resulting random ε as label noise versus weight/feature noise.
Explain the puzzling claim that the Taylor approximation in Eq. 14 'does not get better for larger number of samples' and what its practical role is.
Provide an explicit analog of the smoothing strength α in the induced noise so practitioners can control magnitude.

correct: Theorem 1: for q=N(θ_t, I) and the specified loss, expected gradient equals gradient with label noise ε_{i|t}=σ(f_i(θ_t)) − E_q[σ(f_i(θ))].
Extension to GLMs via A'(·) (Eq. 16) follows directly from the log-partition structure.
VON-as-Newton derivation via Khan et al. (2019) surrogate at ρ_t=1 is standard and sound.
partially_correct: Theorem 2 (Appendix A.1): the surrogate equivalence is correct, but the claimed identification of ε_{i|t} with 'label noise' presumes the same gradient manipulation as Theorem 1; the proof is omitted with the claim it is 'identical', which is acceptable but should be made explicit for the full-covariance case.

NN-case noise expression (Eq. 20): an approximation requiring single-sample MC and first-order Taylor expansion, with second-order terms dropped. This is a heuristic derivation, not a rigorous result; the paper itself notes the Taylor approximation does not improve with more samples.
Claim that second-order methods yield 'more adaptive' label noise: argued informally; not made quantitative.
suggested_improvements_for_theoretical_s: Include a complete proof of Theorem 2 rather than asserting it is identical to Theorem 1.

Provide error bounds on the Taylor approximation used to derive Eq. 20 for the NN case, or empirically validate that Eq. 20 matches the actual difference Eq_t[∇ℓ] − ∇ℓ(θ_t) induced by IVON during training.
Discuss whether the 'label noise' decomposition is unique or whether some of ε_{i|t} should be attributed to feature/weight noise.
Make precise the regularity conditions allowing exchange of expectation and gradient.

supported: IVON outperforms tuned LS on symmetric/pair-flip noise across CIFAR-10/100, with consistent direction across noise levels and 5 seeds (Figs. 5, 8).
Learned Σ_t outperforms fixed Σ_t on CIFAR-100 across three noise settings (Fig. 6).
IVON is comparable to best-tuned SAM in synthetic noise settings, with SAM showing high sensitivity to ρ (Figs. 5, 8).
partially_supported: IVON's induced label noise is 'similar' to OLS (Fig. 4): comparison is purely visual on log-scale histograms for selected classes; no quantitative similarity metric (KL, correlation, rank correlation) is provided.
Variational learning assigns higher noise to atypical examples (Fig. 3): qualitative MNIST visualization only on a small CNN; no quantitative atypicality measure.
Clothing1M improvement over LS (~1%): the LS baseline accuracy appears low relative to literature, weakening the comparison.
'~51% gain' on data-dependent noise: occurs only at high κ where both baselines collapse to ~10%; framing as a general gain is overstated.
unsupported_or_overstated: Claim that IVON 'does not need to tune any hyperparameters' is contradicted by Table 1 (per-setting tuning of lr, Hessian init, weight decay) and Fig. 10 (pair-flip 20% accuracy drops to ~20% at h_0=0.05).
Claim in Fig. 10 caption that IVON varies by 'up to 10%' is inaccurate; the pair-flip 20% curve spans roughly 50 percentage points across h_0 values.
Introduction motivates calibration, OOD detection, and distribution shift, but experiments only address labeling errors; no calibration (ECE/NLL) or OOD evaluation is shown.

OLS is claimed as the most relevant baseline (similarity comparison) but is omitted from the noisy-label accuracy benchmarks, leaving the practical comparison with the conceptually closest method missing.
suggested_improvements_for_claim_support: Add a quantitative similarity metric (KL divergence, correlation, rank agreement) between IVON's induced label distributions and OLS across all classes.
Include OLS (and other adaptive LS methods: Ko et al. 2023, Lee et al. 2022, Park et al. 2023) in the accuracy benchmarks under labeling errors.
Add noise-robust baselines such as GCE, SCE, ELR, Co-teaching, DivideMix, especially on Clothing1M where these are standard.
Report ECE, NLL, and other calibration metrics to support the overconfidence framing in the introduction.
Empirically validate that Eq. 20 actually approximates the realized noise IVON induces in NN training.
Quantify 'atypicality' (e.g., via predictive entropy under a held-out model, human ratings, or distance to class mean) and correlate with IVON noise magnitude.
Test calibration and OOD detection benefits if those motivations are to be retained in the introduction.
Investigate whether stacking LS on top of IVON helps or hurts.

experimental_rigor.baseline_quality: Limited. Only LS and SAM are used as performance baselines under labeling errors; the most conceptually related baseline (OLS) is included only for visual similarity, not accuracy. No comparison to standard noise-robust methods on Clothing1M (where literature accuracy is typically ~70–76%).
missing_baselines: Online Label Smoothing (Zhang et al., 2021) in accuracy experiments.
Other adaptive LS methods (Ko et al. 2023, Lee et al. 2022 ACLS, Park et al. 2023).
Noise-robust learning baselines: GCE, SCE, ELR, Co-teaching, DivideMix, mixup, bootstrap.
Deep ensembles or other Bayesian baselines (MC-Dropout, Laplace approximation) to isolate the contribution of variational learning specifically.
experimental_rigor.dataset_quality: Standard benchmarks (MNIST, CIFAR-10/100, Clothing1M). Clothing1M provides natural noise. The pair-flip class ordering in CIFAR-10 is unusual (sequential class index rather than semantically similar pairs), and the data-dependent noise construction is somewhat arbitrary.
better_datasets: CIFAR-10N/CIFAR-100N (real human label noise) for more realistic noise comparisons.
WebVision or ANIMAL-10N for further natural-noise evaluation.
OOD/calibration benchmarks (CIFAR-10-C, ImageNet-C/R) to support overconfidence claims.
experimental_rigor.metric_appropriateness: Accuracy is appropriate for noisy-label classification but insufficient given the paper's overconfidence/calibration framing.
better_metric: Quantitative similarity metrics (KL, JSD, rank correlation) for the OLS comparison.
Correlation between IVON label noise magnitude and a quantitative atypicality measure.
missing_ablations: Comparison between variational GD (fixed identity covariance) and IVON on the same NN tasks to directly test the 'second-order yields more adaptive noise' claim.
Multi-sample MC ablation versus single-sample for the noise estimate in IVON.
Ablation on number of MC samples used per gradient step.
IVON + LS combination to test orthogonality of effects.
Sensitivity to other IVON hyperparameters (β_2, λ, weight decay) beyond Hessian init.
experimental_rigor.statistical_significance: 5 seeds reported in most plots but no standard deviations, confidence intervals, or significance tests are shown. Improvement margins are stated as point estimates only.
better_significance_test_or_confidence_i: Report mean ± standard deviation or 95% confidence intervals in figures.
Conduct paired statistical tests (e.g., Wilcoxon signed-rank across seeds) for headline comparisons.
Report per-seed accuracy tables in the appendix.
experimental_rigor.hyperparameter_clarity: Hyperparameters are listed in Appendix C and Table 1, but per-setting tuning of lr, Hessian init, and weight decay contradicts the main-text claim of tuning-free behavior. Sweep ranges for LS (α grid) and SAM (ρ grid) are coarse.
possible_confounders: Hyperparameters were tuned differently per noise setting for IVON but not necessarily for LS/SAM, which can bias comparisons.
Choice of pair-flip ordering (sequential index) may not match the convention of literature, affecting comparability of pair-flip results.
Clothing1M LS baseline accuracy seems low compared to literature, suggesting suboptimal training setup that may inflate relative gains.
Single-sample MC estimates of IVON gradients add stochastic noise that may itself act as regularization, beyond the 'adaptive label smoothing' mechanism.
confounder_reduction_alternatives: Use the same hyperparameter tuning protocol (budget, search space, validation) for all methods.
Adopt a standard pair-flip ordering (semantically similar class pairs) as used in prior noisy-label papers.
Compare against published Clothing1M baselines or replicate stronger LS/SAM settings.
Disentangle MC sampling noise from the analytic label-noise mechanism with ablations on sample counts and deterministic mean updates.
suggestions_for_improving_reproducibilit: Provide per-seed numerical results in appendix tables.
Document hyperparameter search protocol explicitly (budget, validation set, selection criterion).
Provide exact training scripts and dataset preparation (especially for synthetic noise generation and pair-flip ordering).

strengths: Clean and intuitive conceptual contribution: framing the expected gradient under a Gaussian variational posterior as inducing per-example adaptive label noise unifies two largely separate literatures (Bayesian deep learning and adaptive label smoothing).
Theorem 1 and its GLM extension (Eqs. 10, 16) are correct, clearly derived, and provide an exact closed-form expression for the induced label noise in the linear/GLM case.

Extension to VON/Newton (Theorem 2, App. A.1) elegantly shows that learning the covariance Σ_t makes the induced noise adaptive in both location and spread, with empirical support from the fixed-vs-learned Σ ablation (Fig. 6).

Empirical results are consistent in direction across multiple noise types (symmetric, pair-flip, data-dependent, natural) and two datasets (CIFAR-10/100, Clothing1M), with IVON outperforming tuned LS and matching tuned SAM, run over 5 seeds.
The data-dependent noise experiment (Fig. 7) is a useful diagnostic and shows striking robustness of IVON in high-noise regimes where LS and SAM collapse.
Hyperparameter sensitivity comparison (Fig. 10 vs SAM's ρ sweeps in Figs. 5, 8, 9) supports the practical claim that IVON is less sensitive to its main tuning knob than SAM.
weaknesses: The most conceptually relevant baseline, Online Label Smoothing (Zhang et al., 2021), is included only for a visual similarity comparison (Fig. 4) but is absent from all accuracy benchmarks. Other adaptive LS methods (Ko et al. 2023, Lee et al. 2022, Park et al. 2023) and standard noise-robust methods (GCE, SCE, ELR, Co-teaching, DivideMix) are not compared at all, despite being natural baselines, especially on Clothing1M.

The neural-network derivation (Eq. 20) relies on single-sample MC plus a first-order Taylor expansion with second-order terms dropped, and is never empirically validated. There is no check that the analytic formula approximates the actual realized noise induced by IVON, and the decomposition into 'label noise' (versus feature/weight noise) is non-unique.

The 'no hyperparameter tuning' claim (Sec. 3.4, 4.3.1) is contradicted by Table 1, which shows per-setting tuning of learning rate, Hessian init, and weight decay. Fig. 10 also shows IVON's accuracy can vary substantially with Hessian init (e.g., pair-flip 20% collapses near h_0=0.05), contrary to its caption.

The introduction motivates miscalibration, OOD detection, and distribution shift, but experiments cover only labeling errors. No calibration metrics (ECE, NLL, Brier) or OOD evaluations are reported despite the overconfidence framing.

Quantitative similarity between IVON's and OLS's smoothed labels (Fig. 4) is shown only via log-scale bar plots for selected classes; no KL/correlation/rank-agreement metric is reported, making the 'surprisingly similar' claim subjective.
Statistical reporting is weak: figures show mean accuracy over 5 seeds but no error bars or confidence intervals, and no paired significance tests for the headline gains.
The 'up to ~51%' gain on data-dependent noise occurs in a regime where baselines collapse to near-chance; framing this as a general benefit is misleading.
Clothing1M LS baseline accuracy (~65–67%) appears low relative to literature, and the gain over LS is only ~1%, suggesting suboptimal baseline tuning may inflate relative improvements.
minor_issues: Theorem 2 proof is asserted to be 'identical' to Theorem 1's; a complete proof for the full-covariance case would be clearer.
Regularity conditions for exchanging expectation and gradient (Eq. 11) are not stated.
Comment that the Taylor approximation 'does not get better for larger number of samples' (after Eq. 14) is confusing and deserves clarification.
Pair-flip class ordering in CIFAR-10 follows class index rather than semantic similarity (the more common convention), which may affect comparability with prior work.
Reproducibility would be substantially improved by releasing code and providing per-seed numerical results.
Several captions describe accuracy variations imprecisely (e.g., Fig. 10).
Typos: 'naturally' → 'natural' in 'variational method natural yields'; 'mislabling'; 'neworks'; 'Pereyra et al. (2017a)' duplicated as (2017b).
questions_for_authors: Why is OLS, the conceptually closest method, omitted from the accuracy benchmarks in Sec. 4.3? Can you add it (and other adaptive LS variants such as ACLS and Ko et al. 2023) to all noisy-label experiments?

Can you empirically verify that Eq. 20 approximates the actual difference E_q[∇ℓ_i] − ∇ℓ_i(θ_t) that IVON induces during NN training, e.g., by comparing realized 'label noise' magnitudes to the analytic prediction?

Given that hyperparameters (lr, Hessian init, weight decay) are tuned per setting in Table 1, how do you reconcile the claim that IVON 'does not require hyperparameter tuning'? Would the LS/SAM gains shrink with comparable per-setting tuning?
Can you report ECE/NLL for IVON, LS, and SAM to substantiate the overconfidence framing?
How sensitive are the smoothed-label-similarity conclusions (Fig. 4) to a quantitative metric such as KL divergence between IVON, OLS, and LS distributions over all classes?
On Clothing1M, why is the LS baseline lower than commonly reported in the noisy-label literature, and how does IVON compare to standard noise-robust baselines (GCE, SCE, ELR, DivideMix)?
Do you have a direct ablation between variational GD (fixed identity covariance) and IVON in the NN setting to support the claim that second-order variational methods are more adaptive?
Does combining LS or OLS with IVON yield further gains, or are the effects redundant?
parsed.recommendation_rationale: The paper makes a clean and useful conceptual connection between variational learning and adaptive label smoothing, with a correct GLM-level derivation and a reasonable (though approximate) extension to neural networks. The empirical results consistently support the practical claim that IVON outperforms tuned LS and matches tuned SAM under various forms of label noise, with the data-dependent noise diagnostic being particularly compelling. However, the contribution is more reframing than fundamentally new theory, the NN-level analysis is heuristic and not empirically validated, the experimental section omits the most relevant adaptive-LS baseline (OLS) and standard noise-robust methods, and several claims (hyperparameter-freedom, calibration/OOD motivations, ~51% gains, OLS similarity) are overstated. These issues are addressable in revision and do not undermine the core contribution. Overall, this is a modest but useful workshop/short-paper-style contribution with above-threshold value for the community.

parsed.summary_of_paper:
The paper draws an explicit connection between variational learning with Gaussian posteriors and adaptive label smoothing. The authors prove that variational GD on logistic regression with q = N(θ_t, I) is exactly equivalent to standard GD with per-example label noise ε_{i|t} = σ(f_i(θ_t)) − E_{q_t}[σ(f_i(θ))] (Theorem 1), extend the result to general GLM losses by replacing σ with the log-partition derivative A' (Eq. 16), and to Newton/VON updates with learned full covariance Σ_t (Theorem 2, App. A.1). For neural networks, they derive an approximate form (Eq. 20) using a single-sample Monte Carlo and first-order Taylor expansion. The induced noise grows for atypical examples whose predictions sit near the decision boundary and have high predictive variance.

Empirically, the authors show that (i) IVON assigns higher label noise to atypical MNIST digits (Fig. 3), (ii) its per-class smoothed-label distributions on CIFAR-10/100 visually resemble Online Label Smoothing (Fig. 4), and (iii) IVON consistently outperforms best-tuned LS and matches/beats best-tuned SAM on CIFAR-10/100 (symmetric, pair-flip, data-dependent noise) and Clothing1M. Ablations show learned Σ_t outperforms a fixed diagonal Σ_t (Fig. 6), and IVON appears less sensitive to its Hessian initialization than SAM is to its perturbation radius (Fig. 10).
main_strengths: Clean conceptual contribution that unifies two largely separate literatures (Bayesian deep learning and adaptive label smoothing) by reinterpreting the expected gradient under a Gaussian posterior as adaptive per-example label noise.
Theorem 1 and the GLM extension (Eqs. 10, 16) are correct, clearly derived, and yield exact closed-form expressions for the induced label noise in linear/GLM settings.

The Newton/VON extension (Theorem 2, App. A.1) elegantly shows that learning Σ_t makes the induced noise adaptive in both location and spread, with empirical support from the fixed-vs-learned Σ ablation (Fig. 6).

Empirical results are consistent in direction across multiple noise types (symmetric, pair-flip, data-dependent, natural) and datasets (CIFAR-10/100, Clothing1M), with comparisons against best-tuned LS and SAM and reporting over 5 seeds.
The data-dependent noise diagnostic (Fig. 7) is a useful experiment showing striking robustness of IVON in high-noise regimes where LS and SAM collapse to near-chance.
The hyperparameter-sensitivity comparison (Fig. 10 vs. SAM's ρ sweeps in Figs. 5, 8, 9) supports the practical claim that IVON is less brittle to its main tuning knob than SAM.
main_weaknesses: The most conceptually relevant baseline, Online Label Smoothing (Zhang et al., 2021), is only included for a visual similarity check (Fig. 4) and absent from all accuracy benchmarks. Other adaptive LS methods (Ko et al. 2023; Lee et al. 2022; Park et al. 2023) and standard noise-robust methods (GCE, SCE, ELR, Co-teaching, DivideMix) are not compared, despite being natural baselines, especially on Clothing1M.

The neural-network derivation (Eq. 20) relies on single-sample MC plus a first-order Taylor expansion with second-order terms dropped, and is never empirically validated. There is no check that the analytic formula approximates the realized noise induced by IVON, and the decomposition into 'label noise' vs. feature/weight noise is non-unique.

The 'no hyperparameter tuning' framing (Sec. 3.4, 4.3.1) is contradicted by Table 1, which shows per-setting tuning of learning rate, Hessian init, and weight decay. Fig. 10 also shows IVON's accuracy varies substantially with Hessian init in some settings (e.g., pair-flip 20% collapses near h_0=0.05), partially contradicting the caption.

The introduction motivates miscalibration, OOD detection, and distribution shift, but the experiments cover only labeling errors. No calibration metrics (ECE, NLL, Brier) or OOD evaluations are reported despite the overconfidence framing.
The 'surprisingly similar' OLS-IVON claim (Fig. 4) is based on log-scale bar plots for selected classes only; no quantitative similarity measure (KL divergence, rank agreement) is provided.
Statistical reporting is weak: figures show mean accuracy over 5 seeds with no error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests.
The headline '~51%' improvement on data-dependent noise occurs in a regime where baselines collapse to ~10% (near chance); presenting this as a general benefit overstates the practical gain.
The Clothing1M LS baseline (~65–67%) is low relative to the noisy-label literature, and the IVON gain over LS is only ~1%, suggesting suboptimal baseline tuning may inflate relative improvements.
detailed_comments: Sec. 3.1: The proof of Theorem 1 is clean, but the regularity conditions for swapping expectation and gradient in Eq. 11 are left implicit. Stating them (e.g., differentiability and dominated convergence) would improve rigor.
Sec. 3.3 / App. A.1: Theorem 2's proof is asserted to be 'identical' to Theorem 1's. Given that Σ_t is now learned and full, a complete derivation (even brief) would make the result self-contained.

Sec. 3.4: The comment after Eq. 14 that the Taylor approximation 'does not get better for larger number of samples' is confusing as stated and deserves clarification—presumably it refers to bias not vanishing rather than variance reduction.

Sec. 4.1, Fig. 3: The MNIST visualization is illustrative but anecdotal. A quantitative measure (e.g., correlation between noise magnitude and predictive entropy, or model confidence on a separately validated 'atypicality' score) would strengthen the claim.

Sec. 4.2, Fig. 4: The OLS-IVON similarity claim would be far more convincing with a quantitative aggregate metric (e.g., mean KL divergence between per-class smoothed-label distributions across all classes, not just selected ones).

Sec. 4.3.1: Pair-flip class ordering in CIFAR-10 appears to follow class index rather than semantic similarity (a more common convention in the noisy-label literature). This may affect comparability with prior work; clarifying this choice is important.

Sec. 4.3.2 / Fig. 7: The construction of P with κ + βi for i ∈ [1, K] means class K-1 has noise level κ + β(K-1), which for κ=0.4 and β=0.05 on CIFAR-10 exceeds 0.85, i.e., the last class is essentially unlearnable. The framing should distinguish gains in moderately noisy regimes from gains in essentially-random-label regimes.

Sec. 4.3.3 / Fig. 9: A ~1% improvement on Clothing1M, where LS appears weakly tuned, is a modest result. Reporting standard noise-robust baselines (e.g., GCE, ELR, DivideMix) would contextualize this.

Fig. 6: The ablation is valuable, but the fixed-Σ baseline could also be shown across multiple variance scales chosen by validation, and the figure would benefit from clarifying which axes correspond to which dataset.
App. B.1: Fig. 10's claim that variation is bounded by 10% is contradicted by the pair-flip 20% curve, which collapses at h_0=0.05. The caption should be revised.
Reproducibility: No code release is mentioned. Per-seed numerical results, code, and exact hyperparameters for all experiments (not just Table 1) would substantially improve reproducibility.
Minor typos: 'variational method natural yields' (should be 'naturally yields'); 'mislabling'; 'neworks'; duplicated Pereyra et al. (2017a/b).
questions_for_authors: Why is Online Label Smoothing—the conceptually closest method—omitted from the accuracy benchmarks in Sec. 4.3? Can you add OLS (and other adaptive LS variants like ACLS and Ko et al. 2023) to all noisy-label experiments?

Can you empirically verify that Eq. 20 approximates the actual difference E_q[∇ℓ_i] − ∇ℓ_i(θ_t) that IVON induces during NN training, e.g., by comparing realized label-noise magnitudes to the analytic prediction across training?

Given the per-setting tuning of learning rate, Hessian init, and weight decay in Table 1, how do you reconcile the claim that IVON 'does not require hyperparameter tuning'? Would the gains over LS/SAM shrink under comparable per-setting tuning of those baselines?
Can you report ECE/NLL/Brier scores for IVON, LS, and SAM to substantiate the overconfidence/calibration framing in the introduction?
How sensitive are the OLS-IVON similarity conclusions (Fig. 4) to quantitative metrics such as mean KL divergence between smoothed-label distributions over all classes?
On Clothing1M, why is the LS baseline lower than commonly reported in the noisy-label literature, and how does IVON compare to standard noise-robust baselines (GCE, SCE, ELR, DivideMix)?

Do you have a direct ablation between variational GD (fixed identity covariance) and IVON in the NN setting to support the claim that second-order variational methods are more adaptive than first-order ones?
Does combining LS or OLS with IVON yield further gains, or are the effects redundant?
What is the rationale for using the class-index-based pair-flip ordering in CIFAR-10 rather than the semantically-motivated pairs used in much prior work?
suggestions_for_improvement: Add accuracy comparisons against OLS and at least one other adaptive LS method (ACLS or Ko et al. 2023) across all noisy-label settings; also include standard noise-robust baselines on Clothing1M.
Empirically validate Eq. 20 by measuring the realized noise during IVON training and comparing it to the analytic prediction; this would substantiate the NN-level theoretical claim.
Soften or qualify the 'no hyperparameter tuning' framing, and clearly disclose tuned hyperparameters (Table 1) in the main text rather than only in the appendix.
Report calibration (ECE, NLL) and—if feasible—an OOD experiment, to back the overconfidence motivation in the introduction.
Replace or supplement Fig. 4 with a quantitative aggregate similarity metric (e.g., KL or correlation) between IVON and OLS smoothed-label distributions across all classes.
Add error bars / confidence intervals and at least one paired significance test for headline gains; report per-seed numbers in the appendix.
Reframe the data-dependent noise result by distinguishing high-noise regimes where baselines reach chance from regimes where they are competitive; consider intermediate κ to show transitions.
Provide a complete proof of Theorem 2 (rather than appealing to Theorem 1) and state the regularity conditions used in Eq. 11.
Release code and exact training configurations to enable reproduction.
Fix typos and the Fig. 10 caption, which currently overstates IVON's robustness in the pair-flip 20% setting.

misrepresentations: The draft says 'figures show mean accuracy over 5 seeds with no error bars'—the paper does not clearly state whether error bars are shown; some figures may include shading. This should be softened to 'error bars/CIs are not clearly visible or reported numerically' rather than asserted as absent.
The critique that the Clothing1M LS baseline is 'low relative to the noisy-label literature' is plausible but not directly verified against numbers in the paper; the wording should be hedged.

The claim that Fig. 10's caption is 'contradicted' by the pair-flip curve overstates: the caption says variation can be up to ~10% when Hessian is bigger than 0.05, and the pair-flip collapse occurs precisely at h_0=0.05, so the caption is technically consistent though misleading. Should be reworded to 'the caption's hedge ‘bigger than 0.05’ obscures a sharp collapse at the boundary'.
missed_strengths: The paper provides a pseudo-code for IVON (Alg. 1) and explicitly identifies which terms in the variational gradient correspond to label noise vs. other noises, aiding interpretability.
The framework unifies multiple prior heuristic adaptive-LS schemes under a single principled objective, which has pedagogical value.
missed_weaknesses: The novelty is somewhat limited: the observation that variational/Bayesian methods produce expected-gradient terms equivalent to soft labels is implicit in prior work on Bayesian deep learning and PAC-Bayes; the paper's framing as 'adaptive label smoothing' is a reinterpretation rather than a new algorithm.
No theoretical analysis of when the induced adaptivity is provably beneficial (e.g., generalization bounds, noise-robustness guarantees).
too_strong_claims: Calling the contribution 'weak accept' with overall=6 while listing many substantive weaknesses (missing key baselines, unvalidated NN derivation, no calibration experiments despite framing) may be slightly generous; however, the conceptual contribution justifies borderline. Consider whether 'borderline' or 5 is more accurate—but 6/weak accept is defensible.
corrections_needed: Soften the statement about absent error bars to acknowledge uncertainty about what is shown in figures.
Hedge the Clothing1M baseline-strength claim ('appears low relative to commonly reported numbers' rather than asserting it).
Reword the Fig. 10 caption critique to note that the caption's qualifier 'bigger than 0.05' technically holds, but is misleading because the collapse occurs at the threshold.
Add a brief note that the novelty is primarily a reinterpretation/unification, not a new algorithm, in the weaknesses.
Consider adding a missed weakness about lack of formal generalization/robustness guarantees.

final_review.summary_of_paper: The paper draws an explicit connection between variational learning with Gaussian posteriors and adaptive label smoothing. The authors prove that variational GD on logistic regression with q = N(θ_t, I) is exactly equivalent to standard GD with per-example label noise ε_{i|t} = σ(f_i(θ_t)) − E_{q_t}[σ(f_i(θ))] (Theorem 1), extend the result to general GLM losses by replacing σ with the log-partition derivative A' (Eq. 16), and to Newton/VON updates with learned full covariance Σ_t (Theorem 2, App. A.1). For neural networks, they derive an approximate form (Eq. 20) using a single-sample Monte Carlo and first-order Taylor expansion. The induced noise grows for atypical examples whose predictions sit near the decision boundary and have high predictive variance. Empirically, the authors show that (i) IVON assigns higher label noise to atypical MNIST digits (Fig. 3), (ii) its per-class smoothed-label distributions on CIFAR-10/100 visually resemble Online Label Smoothing (Fig. 4), and (iii) IVON consistently outperforms best-tuned LS and matches/beats best-tuned SAM on CIFAR-10/100 (symmetric, pair-flip, data-dependent noise) and Clothing1M. Ablations show learned Σ_t outperforms a fixed diagonal Σ_t (Fig. 6), and IVON appears less sensitive to its Hessian initialization than SAM is to its perturbation radius (Fig. 10).
main_strengths: Clean conceptual contribution that unifies two largely separate literatures (Bayesian deep learning and adaptive label smoothing) by reinterpreting the expected gradient under a Gaussian posterior as adaptive per-example label noise, with pedagogical value in subsuming several prior heuristic adaptive-LS schemes under a single principled objective.
Theorem 1 and the GLM extension (Eqs. 10, 16) are correct, clearly derived, and yield exact closed-form expressions for the induced label noise in linear/GLM settings.

The Newton/VON extension (Theorem 2, App. A.1) elegantly shows that learning Σ_t makes the induced noise adaptive in both location and spread, with empirical support from the fixed-vs-learned Σ ablation (Fig. 6).

Empirical results are consistent in direction across multiple noise types (symmetric, pair-flip, data-dependent, natural) and datasets (CIFAR-10/100, Clothing1M), with comparisons against best-tuned LS and SAM and reporting over 5 seeds.
The data-dependent noise diagnostic (Fig. 7) is a useful experiment showing striking robustness of IVON in high-noise regimes where LS and SAM collapse to near-chance.

The hyperparameter-sensitivity comparison (Fig. 10 vs. SAM's ρ sweeps in Figs. 5, 8, 9) supports the practical claim that IVON is less brittle to its main tuning knob than SAM, and the included IVON pseudo-code (Alg. 1) plus an explicit separation of label vs. other induced noises aids interpretability.
main_weaknesses: The most conceptually relevant baseline, Online Label Smoothing (Zhang et al., 2021), is only included for a visual similarity check (Fig. 4) and absent from all accuracy benchmarks. Other adaptive LS methods (Ko et al. 2023; Lee et al. 2022; Park et al. 2023) and standard noise-robust methods (GCE, SCE, ELR, Co-teaching, DivideMix) are not compared, despite being natural baselines, especially on Clothing1M.

The neural-network derivation (Eq. 20) relies on single-sample MC plus a first-order Taylor expansion with second-order terms dropped, and is never empirically validated. There is no check that the analytic formula approximates the realized noise induced by IVON, and the decomposition into 'label noise' vs. feature/weight noise is non-unique.

The 'no hyperparameter tuning' framing (Sec. 3.4, 4.3.1) is in tension with Table 1, which shows per-setting tuning of learning rate, Hessian init, and weight decay for IVON. Fig. 10 also shows IVON's accuracy varies substantially with Hessian init in some settings (e.g., pair-flip 20% collapses near h_0=0.05); the caption's hedge ('bigger than 0.05') is technically consistent but understates this sensitivity at the boundary.

The introduction motivates miscalibration, OOD detection, and distribution shift, but the experiments cover only labeling errors. No calibration metrics (ECE, NLL, Brier) or OOD evaluations are reported despite the overconfidence framing.

The 'surprisingly similar' OLS-IVON claim (Fig. 4) is based on log-scale bar plots for selected classes only; no quantitative similarity measure (KL divergence, rank agreement) is provided across all classes.
Statistical reporting is limited: figures report mean accuracy over 5 seeds but error bars/confidence intervals and significance tests are not clearly visible or reported numerically.

The headline '~51%' improvement on data-dependent noise occurs in a regime where baselines collapse to ~10% (near chance); presenting this as a general benefit may overstate the practical gain in moderately noisy regimes.
On Clothing1M, the IVON gain over LS is only ~1%, and the LS baseline appears low relative to commonly reported numbers in the noisy-label literature, which may inflate the relative improvement.

The novelty is primarily a reinterpretation/unification rather than a new algorithm: that variational/Bayesian objectives induce expected-gradient terms equivalent to soft labels is implicit in prior Bayesian deep learning and PAC-Bayes work. There is also no formal generalization or noise-robustness guarantee establishing when the induced adaptivity is provably beneficial.
